title: Sahel Insurgency Update — Mar 29, 2026
summary: IS-Sahel killed 6 Niger Army soldiers in an attack on positions at Sanama, Tillabéri Region on March 25, reinforcing the group's sustained offensive pressure in western Niger's Liptako-Gourma zone. A new map-line documents the Sanama attack vector. A Malian Armed Forces patrol briefly crossed into Mauritanian territory occupying six villages before withdrawing after diplomatic negotiations — an incident attributed to GPS/border demarcation confusion with no casualties. The JNIM fuel blockade resolution (March 23) was confirmed with diesel prices reaching 940 CFA/liter (+29.7%), updating the contested blockade claim's resolution status. The conflict has now entered its 15th year since the initial MNLA offensive in January 2012.

title: Sahel Insurgency Update — Mar 29, 2026
summary: FAMa conducted a major airstrike on an IS-Sahel base northwest of Ménaka on March 26, claiming 50+ militants neutralized and an entire logistics camp destroyed — the third Malian engagement in the Ménaka theater in one week. IS-Sahel responded on March 27 with a motorcycle ambush on a FAMa patrol at Inkadevan north of Ménaka, with FAMa helicopter gunships striking retreating fighters. On March 22, JNIM killed at least 14 soldiers in an assault on a military post in Bagade, northern Burkina Faso — part of an escalating JNIM offensive that has killed 120+ security forces in Burkina since February. The economic impact of the Mali–JNIM fuel deal became clear on March 28 when Bamako announced diesel prices had surged 29.7% to 940 CFA francs/liter following the end of the blockade. ECOWAS on March 26 appointed former Guinean Prime Minister Lansana Kouyaté as chief mediator to resume dialogue with the AES states, the third such appointment after predecessors failed to achieve a breakthrough.

title: Sahel Insurgency Update — Mar 26, 2026
summary: Mali's military junta struck a highly controversial deal around March 23, releasing over 100 jihadist detainees to end JNIM's seven-month fuel blockade of Bamako — a truce that analysts warn sets a dangerous precedent for economic warfare as a jihadist negotiating tool. Malian Armed Forces (FAMa), supported by Africa Corps air assets, conducted ground and air operations in the Sikasso, Ségou, and Mopti regions on March 22–24, neutralizing multiple militants in coordinated strikes. Guinea ramped up counter-terrorism activity by dismantling a GSIM/JNIM-linked network on March 23–24, arresting at least 11 suspects in eastern border operations and charging individuals tied to online radicalization networks. Analysts issued fresh warnings on March 25 that JNIM's insurgency is spreading southward into coastal West Africa, with active recruitment underway in Benin, Togo, Côte d'Ivoire, and Ghana via Fulani ethnic networks. The JNIM–ISSP inter-jihadist rivalry continued with reported clashes in Burkina Faso as both groups competed for territorial dominance following February's major coordinated offensives.
